The TCO Gauntlet: Why Huang Says No Competitor Will Show Their Numbers

2 min read · 307 words · Deep Dive #150 · 2026-04-20
Source: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist? · KB #18

Jensen Huang makes a bold and specific claim: no competitor — not Google's TPU, not Amazon's Trainium, not any ASIC — has publicly demonstrated better total cost of ownership (TCO) than Nvidia's GPU platform. He points to public benchmarks like MLPerf and third-party tools like InferenceMAX, challenging competitors to submit their results. According to Huang, none have stepped up.

The economics he cites are striking. Nvidia's gross margins sit around 70%, while ASIC margins from companies like Broadcom hover near 65%. Huang argues this narrow gap undermines the entire economic rationale for custom silicon. If the cost savings from switching away from Nvidia are only a few percentage points, the switching costs — rewriting software stacks, losing ecosystem compatibility, sacrificing flexibility for future algorithmic changes — make the trade deeply unfavorable.

Huang frames Nvidia's TCO advantage as a product of extreme co-design across the full stack: processors, NVLink fabric, networking with Spectrum-X, CUDA libraries, and algorithmic optimizations. He claims that Nvidia engineers routinely achieve 2-3x speedups when optimizing partner workloads, and that these gains compound across the fleet. When you're operating hundreds of thousands of GPUs, a 2x improvement in throughput directly doubles effective revenue for the operator.

This argument has a second dimension: performance per watt. In a world where data center capacity is increasingly constrained by energy availability, Huang claims Nvidia delivers the highest tokens-per-watt of any architecture. For a cloud operator building a one-gigawatt facility, choosing the architecture that maximizes token output per watt directly maximizes the lifetime revenue of that investment. Nvidia frames this as a structural advantage that ASICs cannot match because they lack the software-hardware co-design loop.

The TCO gauntlet is strategically clever because it shifts the burden of proof. Rather than defending margins, Huang challenges competitors to prove their alternative delivers superior economics. The silence, he implies, is the answer.
